steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The students claim broken-wing display is exclusive to shorebirds (order Charadriiformes). To weaken this, the data must show at least one non-shorebird performing the behavior.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Unlike the common sandpiper, the South American snipe is not known to perform broken-wing display, even though both are in the order Charadriiformes.’: stays within Charadriiformes; doesn't challenge exclusivity. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Neither the West Peruvian dove nor the South American snipe is known to perform broken-wing display.’: consistent with the claim, not a counterexample. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The common sandpiper, in the order Charadriiformes, is known to perform broken-wing display, and the West Peruvian dove, in the order Columbiformes, is not.’: describes how often shorebirds do it, irrelevant to whether other orders do too. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Both the yellow-billed cuckoo and the sandhill crane are known to perform broken-wing display.’: both are non-shorebirds (Cuculiformes and Gruiformes) that exhibit broken-wing display, directly disproving exclusivity. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
